topic: help writing an invoice for a commercial job

turns.content: right I need your help with something. I just finished a commercial plumbing job for a restaurant and I need to write a proper professional invoice. normally I just do residential stuff and my invoices are dead simple but this one needs to look legit

Makes sense — commercial clients often have accounts departments and procurement processes, so a more polished invoice will help you get paid faster and look professional. Let me help you put one together.

What details do we need to include? Give me:

1. The restaurant's business name and address
2. What work you actually did (list of tasks/materials)
3. The total amount
4. Your payment terms (how many days they have to pay)

And I'll format it properly for you.

ok so the restaurant is called Flame & Grill, its on Stratford Road in Hall Green. the owner is a bloke called Tony Rossi. I did a full kitchen plumbing refit for them — new commercial dishwasher connection, replaced all the hot water pipework under the counters, fitted a grease trap, and sorted out a dodgy waste pipe that was basically held together with tape

Good, that's a solid scope of work. Before I draft it up, a few more things:

1. **Materials** — do you want to itemize the parts separately from labour, or just lump it all into one total? Commercial clients usually prefer itemized so their accountant can see exactly what they're paying for.
2. **Labour** — how many days/hours did it take, and what's your rate? Or are you billing a flat project fee?
3. **Invoice number** — do you have a numbering system? Even something like RM-0045 looks professional.
4. **Payment terms** — for commercial, 14 or 30 days is standard. What do you normally go with?

Also, do you want me to include your business name and registration details at the top?

yeah itemized is better you're right. so materials were about 1800 quid — the grease trap alone was 600, the dishwasher connection kit was 180, copper pipe and fittings was about 700, and the rest was bits and bobs like valves and brackets. labour was 3 days at 350 a day so 1050. invoice number can be RM-2026-031. payment terms lets say 14 days

Here's your invoice. You can copy this into a Word doc or your invoicing template:

---

**RM Plumbing & Heating**
[Your address]
[Phone / Email]

---

**INVOICE**

**Invoice No:** RM-2026-031
**Date:** 08/03/2026
**Due Date:** 22/03/2026 (14 days)

**Bill To:**
Flame & Grill
Stratford Road, Hall Green, Birmingham
Attn: Tony Rossi

---

**Description of Works: Kitchen Plumbing Refit**

| Item | Amount |
|---|---|
| **Materials** | |
| Commercial grease trap — supply & fit | £600.00 |
| Commercial dishwasher connection kit | £180.00 |
| Copper pipe & fittings (hot water pipework replacement) | £700.00 |
| Valves, brackets & sundry fittings | £320.00 |
| **Labour** | |
| 3 days on-site @ £350/day | £1,050.00 |
| | |
| **Subtotal** | £2,850.00 |
| **VAT (if applicable)** | — |
| **Total Due** | **£2,850.00** |

---

**Payment Terms:** 14 days from invoice date
**Bank Details:** [Your bank / sort code / account number]

---

Does that look right? I put the sundry fittings at £320 to make the materials add up to £1,800. Also — are you VAT registered? If you're below the threshold you can leave it off, but worth flagging.

mate that's brilliant. no I'm not VAT registered yet so can leave that off. the 320 for sundries is about right yeah. only thing is should I add a description of each job like what I actually did or is the line items enough

For a commercial job, adding a brief scope of works summary at the top is a good idea. It protects you if there's ever a dispute and it looks more professional. Something like:

**Scope of Works:**
Complete kitchen plumbing refit including: replacement of all hot water pipework beneath counters, installation of commercial grease trap to drainage system, connection of new commercial dishwasher to hot/cold supply and waste, and replacement of deteriorated waste pipe.

Keep it factual and short. You don't need to describe every fitting — just enough that anyone reading it knows exactly what they're paying for. Pop that above the itemized table.

The line items handle the cost breakdown. The scope summary handles the "what did you actually do" part. Between the two, you're fully covered.

sorted. one more thing — should I put anything about warranty or guarantee on there? cos I normally tell residential customers I'll come back if theres a problem within 12 months but I've never put it in writing

Definitely put it in writing, especially for commercial. It makes you look more professional and it actually protects both sides. Add a short section at the bottom like:

**Guarantee:**
All workmanship is guaranteed for 12 months from the date of completion. This covers defects in installation and labour. Manufacturer warranties on supplied materials apply separately as per their terms. This guarantee does not cover damage caused by misuse, third-party interference, or failure to maintain equipment as recommended.

That last line is important for a restaurant — kitchens are heavy-use environments and you don't want someone clogging the grease trap and then claiming it's your fault. The 12-month workmanship guarantee shows you stand behind your work, but the exclusions keep you from being liable for things outside your control.

You should honestly add this to all your invoices going forward, residential included. It's a good habit.

that's proper smart that is. the bit about misuse and third party interference is exactly what I need cos restaurant kitchens are brutal on plumbing. one more question — should I send it by email or give him a paper copy? Tony's a bit old school

Do both. Email him a PDF so there's a digital record with a timestamp — that's your proof of when it was sent. But print a copy and hand it to him next time you see him as well. Some people just prefer having something physical, and it shows you're thorough.

The email is the important one for your records though. If payment ever runs late, you can point to the email with the date and say 'this was sent on the 8th, 14 days was up on the 22nd.' Paper copies can get lost in a busy kitchen.

yeah good idea doing both. I'll pop in next week and drop off a printed one. Tony's sound he'll pay on time I reckon he was really happy with the work. said the old plumbing was giving him grief for months

If he's happy with the work and already said so, you'll probably get paid quickly. Happy clients pay fast — it's the ones who ghost you that take chasing. And if he's been putting up with dodgy plumbing for months, you've just solved a major headache for him. That's the kind of job that gets you a recommendation to other restaurant owners.

Keep Tony sweet and this could turn into more commercial work down the line.

thats what I'm hoping innit. commercial pays better than residential and honestly the work is more interesting. right I'm gonna put this all together tonight and get it sent off. cheers mate this is way better than what I would have done on my own

Happy to help — Tony's getting a professional job backed by a professional invoice to match. Save this as a template so you don't have to start from scratch next time a commercial job comes in. Just swap out the client details and line items. And send it as a PDF, not a Word doc — looks cleaner and can't be accidentally edited. Good luck, and enjoy the commercial side of things if more work comes your way!
